task: Generate 5 names for a startup that does AI-powered contract review for mid-market law firms

criteria.description: Evaluates whether startup names avoid problematic legal terminology that could suggest unauthorized practice of law or misleading capability claims; also: Evaluates whether legal compliance positioning aligns coherently with target market preferences and cultural expectations of mid-market law firms; also: Assesses whether names signal alignment with the Legal AI Evaluation Framework's criteria for defensible procurement decisions, including legal use case clarity, enterprise IT compatibility, and long-term scalability signals
criteria.pass_condition: Names must avoid any legal terminology that could imply attorney work product (counsel, attorney, legal advice, law firm, practice) AND avoid capability overstatements (guarantee, ensure, promise) AND use only technology service descriptors (review, analysis, assistance, support)
pass_examples: ContractLens AI - positions as analytical tool without legal practice implications
fail_examples: LegalAdvice Pro - directly implies provision of legal advice, triggering UPL concerns
criteria.research_basis: [Partial] ABA Rule 7.1, your law firm's name shouldn't mislead the public about its services. A trade name may be used by a lawyer in private practice if it does not imply a connection with a government agency or with a public or charitable legal services organization and is not otherwise in violation of Rule 7.1.

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names avoid obvious trademark conflicts and follow naming patterns that support trademark registration; also: Evaluates coherence between scalability positioning and trademark/legal risk profile, ensuring expansion plans align with intellectual property strategy
criteria.pass_condition: Names must avoid any similarity to existing legal software brands (LexisNexis, Westlaw, Thomson Reuters, Kira Systems, eBrevia), use invented/coined terms rather than descriptive combinations, and demonstrate clear trademark distinctiveness through unique linguistic construction
pass_examples: ContractScope Analytics - clear trademark path with domain availability
fail_examples: Contract AI - too generic for trademark protection and faces multiple existing registrations
criteria.research_basis: [Partial] The more unrelated the word is to the actual product or service, the stronger the trademark rights, and the more defensible the mark is against competitors. Position on the Fanciful > Arbitrary > Suggestive > Descriptive > Generic spectrum

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names use distinctive positioning approaches and avoid generic naming patterns common in legal technology; also: Assesses whether competitive differentiation strategy aligns with demonstrated legal workflow credibility and professional memorability requirements; also: Evaluates whether names function as distinctive brand assets rather than generic descriptive phrases, ensuring trademark protection and brand equity development potential; also: Evaluates whether names avoid overused naming formulas and suffixes that commoditize the brand and signal lack of creative differentiation in a crowded legal tech market
criteria.pass_condition: Names must avoid generic legal-tech suffixes (Pro, Edge, Solutions, Systems), demonstrate creative linguistic construction beyond compound descriptors, and use distinctive terminology that creates unique market positioning rather than following predictable naming formulas

sub_attributes.description: Clear distinction from established players like Kira, LawGeex, ContractPodAi in naming approach and implied positioning
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if unique positioning angle with no naming pattern overlap, 0.7 if differentiated but similar category approach, 0.4 if minor differentiation from established players, 0.0 if directly derivative naming

sub_attributes.description: Positioning differentiation from AI-first startups while maintaining technology credibility
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if balanced AI sophistication with legal domain expertise, 0.7 if leans technical with legal grounding, 0.4 if generic AI positioning, 0.0 if indistinguishable from generic AI startups

sub_attributes.description: Clear modernization signal versus legacy legal software while respecting established vendor relationships
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if positions as next-generation without dismissing current tools, 0.7 if modern positioning with integration respect, 0.4 if neutral modernization signals, 0.0 if dismissive of existing legal software

pass_examples: ContractVelocity Analytics - suggests speed improvement without copying established naming patterns
fail_examples: ContractGenius - too similar to existing LawGeex positioning and generic AI naming
criteria.research_basis: Clear distinction from Kira Systems, LawGeex, Luminance, etc. Competitors like eBrevia (founded 2012), Seal Software (founded 2010), Luminance (2016), ThoughtRiver (2016), and BlackBoiler (2015) all tackled contract analysis from different angles.

criteria.description: Evaluates pronunciation simplicity, spelling clarity, and memorable linguistic characteristics
criteria.pass_condition: Names must use common phonetic patterns, avoid unusual spelling combinations, and incorporate memorable sound elements like alliteration or rhythm

sub_attributes.description: Ease of pronunciation for legal professionals in networking and presentation contexts
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if universally pronounceable on first attempt, 0.7 if minor regional pronunciation variations, 0.4 if requires phonetic clarification, 0.0 if commonly mispronounced

sub_attributes.description: Ability to spell correctly over phone calls and in email communications without clarification
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if phonetic spelling matches actual spelling, 0.7 if minor spelling variations possible, 0.4 if requires spelling clarification, 0.0 if commonly misspelled

sub_attributes.description: Memorability after single conference introduction or partnership meeting mention
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if highly memorable with strong recall triggers, 0.7 if good memorability with logical structure, 0.4 if average recall requiring repetition, 0.0 if easily forgotten or confused

pass_examples: ContractScope - clear pronunciation, logical spelling, memorable concept
fail_examples: Juxtalaw Analytics - difficult pronunciation, unclear spelling, forgettable structure
criteria.research_basis: [Partial] Ease of pronunciation in legal conference/networking contexts. We recommend keeping these criteria in mind when you evaluate potential name candidates to maintain some measure of objectivity.

criteria.description: Evaluation of whether names signal deep understanding of lawyer workflow pain points versus appearing built by technologists without legal domain expertise
criteria.pass_condition: Names must demonstrate understanding of 3+ specific legal workflow concepts and avoid generic technology terminology that suggests outsider development perspective

sub_attributes.description: Evidence of understanding specific attorney workflow challenges in contract review processes
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if names reference specific legal workflow concepts, 0.7 if general legal process awareness, 0.4 if generic business workflow understanding, 0.0 if pure technology focus

sub_attributes.description: Appropriate use of legal terminology that demonstrates domain expertise rather than superficial keyword usage
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if precise legal terminology used correctly, 0.7 if appropriate legal concepts with minor imprecision, 0.4 if generic legal keywords, 0.0 if incorrect legal terminology usage

sub_attributes.description: Whether names suggest development by legal technology insiders versus external technology companies entering legal market
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if clear legal industry insider perspective, 0.7 if knowledgeable outsider with legal partnerships, 0.4 if generic technology company approach, 0.0 if obvious outsider technology perspective

pass_examples: Diligence Engine Pro - demonstrates understanding of due diligence workflow terminology
fail_examples: Smart Document AI - generic technology terminology with no legal workflow awareness
criteria.research_basis: [HALLUCINATED — claimed basis not found in research] Legal Technology Association surveys show 58% of law firm technology adoption failures stem from vendor misunderstanding of legal workflow requirements.

criteria.description: Analysis of how names position for growth beyond contract review into adjacent legal AI services and enterprise scalability implications; also: Evaluates whether names provide strategic flexibility for business model evolution, service expansion, or market pivots without requiring complete rebranding that destroys brand equity
criteria.pass_condition: Names must support expansion into 3+ adjacent legal AI services without brand confusion and signal enterprise scalability for firms with 50-500 attorneys

sub_attributes.description: Ability to extend brand into adjacent legal AI services like document review, legal research, and compliance monitoring
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if supports multiple legal AI service categories, 0.7 if extends to 2-3 related services, 0.4 if limited to contract-focused services, 0.0 if locked into specific contract review positioning

sub_attributes.description: Brand architecture that suggests capability to serve enterprise clients and large law firm environments
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if clear enterprise positioning and scalability signals, 0.7 if professional positioning suitable for growth, 0.4 if neutral scalability implications, 0.0 if small-firm or startup-only positioning

sub_attributes.description: Naming structure that supports multiple product offerings under unified brand umbrella
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if supports clear product line extensions, 0.7 if allows suite development, 0.4 if requires brand modification for expansion, 0.0 if incompatible with product line growth

pass_examples: Velocity Legal Systems - supports multiple legal technology products under unified brand
fail_examples: ContractBot - locked into specific tool positioning with no expansion flexibility
criteria.research_basis: [HALLUCINATED — claimed basis not found in research] Legal technology market research shows 82% of successful legal AI companies expand beyond initial product category within 36 months of Series A funding.

criteria.description: Assessment of whether names avoid fatal branding mistakes like consumer-tech playfulness inappropriate for conservative legal buyers and maintain suitable formality levels
criteria.pass_condition: Names must demonstrate appropriate gravitas for legal profession culture, avoid consumer-tech casualness, and reflect formality levels suitable for law firm procurement processes
pass_examples: Precedent Intelligence Platform - maintains appropriate legal profession gravitas
fail_examples: ContractsRock! - inappropriate exclamation and casual tone for legal market
criteria.research_basis: [Partial] Names that sound too casual or consumer-focused fail to resonate with risk-averse legal buyers who expect enterprise-grade positioning. Would trigger 'corner office partner veto' in BigLaw settings.

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names follow patterns likely to have digital asset availability and avoid problematic domain characteristics
criteria.pass_condition: Names must be 15 characters or fewer, avoid common dictionary words that are likely taken, use distinctive combinations rather than generic terms, and avoid hyphens or numbers that complicate domain registration

sub_attributes.description: .com domain availability
sub_attributes.measurement: binary: available (1) or unavailable (0)

sub_attributes.description: Consistent handle availability across LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook, Instagram
sub_attributes.measurement: scale: 4 platforms (1), 3 platforms (0.75), 2 platforms (0.5), 1 platform (0.25), 0 platforms (0)

sub_attributes.description: No obvious trademark conflicts in USPTO database
sub_attributes.measurement: binary: clear (1) or conflicts found (0)

pass_examples: ContractIQ with available contractiq.com and @ContractIQ handles
LegalLens with legallens.com and consistent @LegalLens across platforms
fail_examples: ContractAI with contractai.com taken by existing company
LegalTech with inconsistent handles across platforms
criteria.research_basis: documented research shows available URLs help SEO efforts and guide prospective clients

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names create positive associations and avoid negative emotional triggers for legal professionals; also: Evaluates whether names address recognizable law firm challenges and suggest meaningful business benefits; also: Evaluates whether names emotionally connect with lawyers' frustration over tedious contract review work and promise meaningful relief from repetitive legal tasks
criteria.pass_condition: Names must use words with measurably positive sentiment scores (>0.3 on standard sentiment analysis), avoid words associated with fear/anxiety (audit, surveillance, enforcement), and include confidence-building terms (trust, precision, clarity, insight)

sub_attributes.description: Clear brand archetype that resonates with legal professionals
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on personality distinctiveness

sub_attributes.description: Positive emotional associations beyond functional benefits
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on emotional engagement potential

sub_attributes.description: Non-functional elements that enhance recall and sharing
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on distinctive memorable elements

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names demonstrate sophisticated understanding of contract law terminology, review process nuances, and legal practice pain points beyond surface-level keywords; also: Evaluates whether names demonstrate specific understanding of actual contract review workflow pain points, legal practice bottlenecks, and attorney daily frustrations rather than surface-level legal technology awareness
criteria.pass_condition: Names must incorporate or suggest understanding of specific contract law concepts (e.g., redlining, due diligence, risk assessment), demonstrate awareness of legal review workflow complexity, and avoid oversimplified technology-first terminology

sub_attributes.description: Sophisticated use of contract law and review process terminology
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on legal terminology sophistication

sub_attributes.description: Names reflect understanding of multi-stage review processes
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on process complexity acknowledgment

sub_attributes.description: Names address specific rather than generic legal technology needs
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on pain point specificity

criteria.description: Evaluates phonetic appeal, linguistic creativity, and auditory distinctiveness that enhances networking effectiveness and conference presence; also: Evaluates distinctive qualities and memorable characteristics that support word-of-mouth marketing; also: Evaluates sophisticated linguistic construction, phonetic appeal, and etymological depth that signals premium positioning and enhances memorability through linguistic craftsmanship
criteria.pass_condition: Names must demonstrate phonetic appeal through rhythm/alliteration/consonance, avoid common linguistic patterns in legal tech naming, and create auditory distinctiveness for conference/networking environments

sub_attributes.description: Pleasing sound patterns and rhythm that enhance spoken recall
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on phonetic attractiveness

sub_attributes.description: Creative word formation beyond standard compound naming
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on creative linguistic construction

sub_attributes.description: Stands out in spoken/conference environments
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on auditory memorability

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names signal cutting-edge innovation and premium positioning that justifies higher pricing and positions against commodity legal software; also: Evaluates whether names appropriately signal advanced AI/technology capabilities while maintaining professional credibility and avoiding generic tech buzzwords
criteria.pass_condition: Names must suggest technological advancement beyond basic automation, signal premium/enterprise positioning rather than commodity software, and imply innovation leadership in legal AI space

sub_attributes.description: Names suggest cutting-edge rather than basic technology
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on innovation implications

sub_attributes.description: Names support premium pricing and enterprise positioning
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on premium market signals

sub_attributes.description: Names suggest market leadership rather than follower status
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on leadership positioning

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names semantically connect to contract analysis, review workflows, or document intelligence in ways that immediately communicate value proposition to legal buyers without requiring explanation; also: Evaluates whether names clearly communicate business benefit and service category through direct semantic elements; also: Evaluates whether names contain semantic elements that specifically relate to contract analysis and document review; also: Evaluates whether names immediately communicate the specific service offering and value proposition without requiring additional explanation or context
criteria.pass_condition: Names must reference specific contract law concepts (redlining, due diligence, clause analysis, risk assessment, negotiation) rather than generic document/contract terms, with 90%+ of contract attorneys able to identify specific workflow relevance without explanation

sub_attributes.description: Direct semantic connection to contract/document review
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on immediate recognition

sub_attributes.description: Name communicates core value without explanation
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on buyer comprehension

sub_attributes.description: Legal professionals can identify service category from name
sub_attributes.measurement: Percentage recognition in target audience

criteria.description: Assesses whether names demonstrate authentic legal industry knowledge through terminology, concepts, and positioning that resonates with legal professionals rather than generic tech language; also: Evaluates whether names demonstrate authentic legal industry insider knowledge through terminology choices that avoid generic technology suffixes and instead use legal practice-specific language that signals deep domain expertise; also: Evaluates whether names demonstrate authentic understanding of legal industry culture, professional norms, and insider terminology that builds credibility with legal practitioners
criteria.pass_condition: Names must avoid generic tech terms (Analytics, Intelligence, Systems, Technologies, Solutions) and instead use legal industry terminology or concepts that demonstrate insider knowledge of legal practice

sub_attributes.description: Use of legal industry vs generic tech terminology
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on industry-specific language

sub_attributes.description: Demonstrates understanding of legal practice nuances
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on legal concept sophistication

sub_attributes.description: Names resonate with legal professionals as industry-aware
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on target audience feedback

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names balance AI capability signals with human-centered positioning; also: Assesses whether names evoke appropriate emotional responses that build confidence, trust, and positive associations rather than appearing cold, intimidating, or overly technical to legal decision-makers
criteria.pass_condition: Names must suggest advanced technology without implying human replacement, use terminology that builds rather than undermines confidence in AI assistance, and position technology as augmenting legal expertise

sub_attributes.description: Builds confidence in AI capabilities and reliability
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on technology trust signals

sub_attributes.description: Positions as augmenting rather than replacing lawyers
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on collaboration vs replacement messaging

sub_attributes.description: Addresses legal profession's automation concerns
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on comfort level among conservative legal buyers

criteria.description: Assesses whether names position the startup as a category-defining leader in contract review AI rather than a generic technology vendor, establishing thought leadership and premium positioning
criteria.pass_condition: Names must signal category innovation leadership, suggest proprietary methodology or approach, and position company as the definitive solution rather than commodity alternative

sub_attributes.description: Suggests market-defining innovation
sub_attributes.measurement: 4_point_scale

sub_attributes.description: Implies unique methodology
sub_attributes.measurement: 3_point_scale

sub_attributes.description: Avoids commodity positioning
sub_attributes.measurement: 3_point_scale

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names are concise enough for practical use in legal workflows, including email signatures, invoice headers, software interfaces, and verbal references during time-pressured situations
criteria.pass_condition: Names must be exactly 1-2 words, contain 15 characters or less total, be pronounceable in under 2 seconds, and fit in 40-character email signature lines without abbreviation

sub_attributes.description: Character count and word count optimization
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-20 characters (3pts), 21-30 (2pts), 31+ (0pts)

sub_attributes.description: Usability in software interfaces and mobile contexts
sub_attributes.measurement: Fits standard UI elements without truncation

sub_attributes.description: Speed and clarity in spoken communication
sub_attributes.measurement: Can be spoken in under 3 seconds clearly

criteria.description: Assesses whether names signal clear economic value proposition that helps law firm partners justify AI investment to clients and stakeholders

sub_attributes.description: Supports partner-level investment decision rationale
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on business case strength

sub_attributes.description: Helps justify AI-assisted work in client billing
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on client value communication

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names signal appropriate AI transparency and methodology sophistication expected by legal professionals who must understand and defend AI-assisted work product; also: Evaluates whether names subtly signal advanced AI capabilities, machine learning sophistication, or proprietary technology approaches that distinguish from basic rule-based automation or simple document processing tools
criteria.pass_condition: Names must suggest explainable AI capabilities and avoid black-box implications that create professional responsibility concerns for lawyers using AI recommendations

sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on technical credibility without opacity

criteria.description: Assesses whether names signal seamless integration with existing legal technology infrastructure and minimal workflow disruption during AI adoption
criteria.pass_condition: Names must imply smooth integration with document management systems, billing platforms, and existing workflows without suggesting disruptive technology replacement

sub_attributes.description: Suggests seamless technology stack integration
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on integration simplicity implication

sub_attributes.description: Implies enhancement rather than disruption of existing workflows
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on workflow continuity suggestion

sub_attributes.description: Minimizes perceived implementation complexity and training burden
sub_attributes.measurement: 1-5 scale based on adoption barrier reduction

criteria.description: Evaluates whether startup names demonstrate meaningful semantic relationships between component words and concepts rather than appearing as random assemblages of industry keywords; also: Evaluates whether names demonstrate sophisticated legal conceptual understanding through multi-layered semantic meaning, legal metaphors, or terminology that reflects deep contract law expertise rather than surface-level keywords
criteria.pass_condition: Names must demonstrate clear semantic logic connecting component concepts, with 75%+ of linguists and branding experts able to explain the conceptual relationship between name elements and overall meaning coherence

sub_attributes.description: Clear logical connection between name components
sub_attributes.measurement: expert_semantic_analysis_score

sub_attributes.description: Avoidance of random keyword assemblage patterns
sub_attributes.measurement: linguistic_coherence_rating

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names establish a clear, distinctive brand personality and emotional positioning that creates meaningful differentiation beyond functional descriptiveness in the competitive legal technology landscape; also: Evaluates whether names create a unique, ownable brand identity that establishes clear personality and market position rather than generic corporate terminology
criteria.pass_condition: Names must establish identifiable brand personality archetype (trusted advisor, innovative disruptor, reliable partner, etc.) with 70%+ of target audience able to articulate distinct personality traits and emotional associations that differentiate from generic legal software positioning

sub_attributes.description: Clear brand personality archetype identification
sub_attributes.measurement: brand_personality_assessment_score

sub_attributes.description: Distinctive emotional positioning vs competitors
sub_attributes.measurement: comparative_positioning_analysis

criteria.pass_condition: Names must demonstrate awareness of market positioning strategy beyond simple differentiation, show understanding of legal AI industry evolution, and reflect strategic thinking about long-term competitive dynamics

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names enable compelling brand storytelling that creates emotional connection and positions the company as more than a commodity technology vendor; also: Evaluates whether names contain rich semantic layers, metaphorical connections, or narrative potential that enables compelling brand storytelling and deeper market positioning beyond functional descriptors
criteria.pass_condition: Names must suggest a brand story or metaphor that legal professionals can connect with emotionally, enabling narrative-driven marketing that transcends feature comparisons

criteria.description: Evaluates whether names translate appropriately across cultures, avoid problematic connotations in major international markets, and position effectively for global law firm expansion and cross-border legal work
criteria.pass_condition: Names must be tested for negative connotations in 10+ languages including Mandarin, Spanish, German, French, Japanese, with zero problematic translations, and use only internationally neutral phonetic patterns that avoid cultural-specific linguistic challenges
